video_title: Franklin Parlamis, Founder & CIO, Aequim Alternative Investments
gist: Franklin Parlamis, CIO of convertible-arbitrage fund Aequim, walks through the credit-vs-equity-vol arbitrage at the heart of converts and warns that today's tight credit spreads plus very high single-name implied vols (AI/data-center names, Oracle, CoreWeave) make the trade far more speculative than the wide-spread, high-vol setup of 2009. He flags contingent-option credit risk, an unexplained shift to inverse skew, and argues the way to trade an uncertain vol regime is a portfolio of asymmetric bets rather than a directional call on whether single-name vol is permanently higher.

insights.claim: In the 1998 Russian default, hedge funds that bought high-yield ruble GKOs and hedged into dollars via currency forwards were secretly relying on an anti-correlation (ruble devaluation offsetting bond losses); the trade blew up because the counterparty banks providing the currency hedges went bankrupt.
insights.speaker: Franklin Parlamis
insights.so_what: A 'hedge' that quietly depends on a correlation assumption is really leverage; counterparty solvency can be the hidden factor that turns a hedged book into a directional blowup.

insights.claim: Arbitrage traders justify leverage by an assumed correlation or anti-correlation between two related assets, and the more confidence they have in that structure the bigger the position gets, so even a small breakdown in the correlation can blow up the volatility of the pair.
insights.speaker: Franklin Parlamis
insights.so_what: Position size scales with correlation confidence; stress-test the pair's vol for correlation breaks, not just the level mispricing.

insights.claim: Convertible arbitrage depends on an intact market 'machine' — the ability to rehypothecate long convertibles and to borrow stock; both broke in the 2008 GFC, forcing leveraged arb players who cared about the arbitrage (not the floor value) to liquidate.
insights.speaker: Franklin Parlamis
insights.so_what: Convert arb has multiple single points of failure (financing, stock loan, credit, equity/rate vol); when leveraged plumbing breaks the asset class can dislocate regardless of fundamentals.
insights.quote: It was the machine broke. And when the machine breaks and the machine has leverage on it, you get that kind of behavior.

insights.claim: Managers who performed well into the 2008 stress were psychologically encouraged to step into more risk as competitors failed, but September-October 2008 was mayhem where only liquidity mattered — a 'winner's curse' where being right earlier just meant owning more at the true crisis low.
insights.speaker: Franklin Parlamis
insights.so_what: Outperforming into a crisis can bait you into adding risk right before the final flush; survival depends on liquidity, not on having been right.

insights.claim: Pine River (under Brian Taylor) treated the money as investor capital and returned it on demand in late 2008 even at humiliating realized losses, delevering to get 'on sides,' and then had a strong 2009 — whereas managers who gated simply marked positions down slowly instead of aggressively.
insights.speaker: Franklin Parlamis
insights.so_what: Right-sizing at the bottom, even at ugly prints, positioned funds to profit in the 2009 recovery; gating deferred but did not avoid the losses.

insights.claim: Convertible bonds are a 'dilettante's discipline' sitting at the intersection of rates, equity vol, credit, credit vol and financing — you don't need to be great at any one thing but many contributing factors means many things can go wrong at once.
insights.speaker: Franklin Parlamis
insights.so_what: The multi-disciplinary nature is both the edge and the tail risk: a confluence of several markets deteriorating (as in the GFC) hits converts hardest.

insights.claim: The core survival rule of arbitrage investing is to treat your own judgment as just one more variable that can be wrong; if you trade with that humility you will always size to survive, because the ability to stay in the game is the single most important thing.
insights.speaker: Franklin Parlamis
insights.so_what: Size positions so that being wrong doesn't force you out; staying solvent is a precondition to capturing any edge.
insights.quote: the ability to stay in is the literal most important thing um in the game.

insights.claim: Even at the GFC lows there was a real downside case (collapse of the entire financial system); markets mostly resolve to the upside but that outcome was not predestined, so any 'I know this is too cheap' conviction should trigger a hubris check.
insights.speaker: Franklin Parlamis
insights.so_what: Hindsight makes crisis lows look obvious; guard against sizing as if a cheap price guarantees the good outcome.

insights.claim: Taking credit risk (owning a high-yield bond) is historically the richest form of short volatility to sell — a better money-making short-vol expression than any tail-selling strategy in listed options — because high-yield returns have proven to be a durable short-vol premium.
insights.speaker: Franklin Parlamis
insights.so_what: Frame credit exposure as short vol; the convert arb sells that rich credit vol and buys cheap vol via the embedded convertible option.

insights.claim: The 'surplus vol' you pay in a convert depends entirely on how wide the credit spread is: at a 1,200-over credit, buying 80 vol leaves modest surplus vol because credit earnings pay down your Vega, but at a 100-over credit, buying 80 vol means roughly 70 of the 80 points is surplus vol you must realize yourself.
insights.speaker: Franklin Parlamis
insights.so_what: Today's tight spreads mean the vol you buy in converts is almost all surplus vol that must be realized through gamma trading, not subsidized by credit carry — a much lower-quality trade than in 2009.
insights.quote: if a credit's 100 over, like many many credits we're trading today are, and you're buying 80 V, 70 of the 80 is the surplus vault, right?

insights.claim: Credit spreads are mostly an expression of risk premium rather than of true default likelihood — high-yield prices do not imply companies will actually default at the implied rate; you are being paid a significant premium simply for the act of taking credit risk.
insights.speaker: Franklin Parlamis
insights.so_what: When credit spreads are wide, risk premia are wide across all asset classes simultaneously, which is why wide-spread regimes are the best entry points for converts and for straightforward risk alike.
insights.quote: credit spreads I think are mostly an expression of risk premium as opposed to an expression of of of true default likelihood

insights.claim: For long-dated volatility (e.g. a five-year call, which is what trades in converts) the risk-premium component historically dominates the Vega effect, so a vol spike that should raise implied from 40 to 45-50 can leave the long-dated option trading around 37 or 35 as its risk premium widens — the opposite of short-dated options, which trade rich.
insights.speaker: Franklin Parlamis
insights.so_what: You can buy long-dated convert vol at a discount to forward expected realized vol even during vol spikes, whereas short-dated listed options stay rich; the two ends of the term structure behave oppositely.
insights.quote: the risk premium component will has at least historically tended to dominate the Vega effect

insights.claim: AI and data-center builders (e.g. CoreWeave) have become the aggressive convertible issuers because a multi-year buildout with no revenue for years can't service much 9% fixed debt but can issue 2% convertibles, a large interest saving they achieve by selling their stock at a premium through the option market.
insights.speaker: Franklin Parlamis
insights.so_what: The current convert supply is concentrated in pre-cash-flow AI/data-center names, so the asset class's risk profile is tied to whether the AI buildout pays off.

insights.claim: A higher interest-rate environment drives more convertible issuance because the zero coupon floor makes the savings versus straight debt larger (an issuer saving ~6% at 5% rates vs ~2% at 1% rates), and a bigger convert asset class carries more liquidity and more distant tail risk than a small one with few players.
insights.speaker: Franklin Parlamis
insights.so_what: Higher rates are structurally bullish for convert supply, opportunity set and liquidity — a positive for arbitrageurs relative to the low-rate 2021 regime.

insights.claim: The AI/data-center issuers are companies with roughly two-year track records and 100-vol stocks that the market is still using to figure out whether the AI thesis works, producing extreme short-term realized vol but no long history to anchor five-year option pricing.
insights.speaker: Franklin Parlamis
insights.so_what: There is no reliable base rate for these names' long-run vol, so five-year convert options are being priced on thin, unstable observables.

themes.theme: Contingent options and credit-vol correlation risk

insights.claim: The long-dated option embedded in a convertible is contingent on the issuer not going bankrupt — it is struck at the conversion price, so if the issuer defaults you never actually owned the option you thought you had; converts only reliably 'work' in upside volatility because rallies never force you to test the credit hypothesis.
insights.speaker: Franklin Parlamis
insights.so_what: The seemingly clean long-vol payoff of a convert is actually a credit-contingent option; a downside test of these AI issuers could break the arb precisely when you most want the vol.
insights.quote: converts always work in upside volatility because you're not being forced to test your credit hypothesis

insights.claim: A selloff in the data-center space — which Parlamis says feels like it may be starting ('physics happening a little bit right now') — would finally test whether these issuers are creditworthy and is where the current convertible arbitrages could break down.
insights.speaker: Franklin Parlamis
insights.so_what: Watch for the first real drawdown in AI/data-center names as the trigger that stress-tests convert credit assumptions and unwinds crowded arb positions.

insights.claim: Managers are being asked to price five-year convertible vol off one-and-a-half-year observables like 2028 LEAPs, while the entire listed option market covers only roughly 2-5% of the Vega sold into the market by the convertible bonds — nowhere near enough to be the dominant hedging venue.
insights.speaker: Franklin Parlamis
insights.so_what: The Vega supplied by converts dwarfs listed-option liquidity, so hedging is imperfect and the forward-vol pricing is closer to guesswork than to a clean executable arbitrage.

insights.claim: Forward realized volatility for these 80-to-120-vol names is not smooth and should be modeled like the jump component of a jump-diffusion equity model — regimes displace discontinuously (a name is either 90 vol or 50 vol, not gliding between them).
insights.speaker: Franklin Parlamis
insights.so_what: Pricing these converts on smooth vol-path assumptions understates gap risk; the real exposure is to discrete regime jumps in single-name vol.

insights.claim: If this cycle ends with established low-vol investment-grade issuers (Parlamis uses Microsoft as a hypothetical) absorbing 80-to-100-vol startups for stock, the back-end Vega of convert arbitrage is impaired — but today's very tight credit spreads leave no room for the offsetting credit tightening that would normally cushion that Vega hit.
insights.speaker: Franklin Parlamis
insights.so_what: Stock-for-stock M&A by IG acquirers is a hidden risk to convert vol books, and the usual credit-spread offset is unavailable when spreads are already tight — an asymmetric downside.

insights.claim: The old world of reliably 25-30 vol 'low-vol' tech leaders no longer exists — Oracle is now roughly a 55-vol stock after deliberately changing its business plan — so managers must guess where a name's vol truly sits rather than rely on ten years of historical realized vol.
insights.speaker: Franklin Parlamis
insights.so_what: Historical vol anchors are broken for tech; even mega-caps can re-rate to high-vol names, undermining assumptions baked into existing convert positions.

insights.claim: A convertible is good or bad depending on whether the issuer's legacy business provides an 'asset floor' that buffers credit and equity on the downside; a single-drug phase-three biotech convert is a binary bet with no credit floor, whereas a biotech with three cash-flowing drugs chasing a fourth has a floor that protects recovery value.
insights.speaker: Franklin Parlamis
insights.so_what: The key screen for a convert is downside recovery value: prefer issuers whose existing business floors the credit, and apply the same asset-floor test to Oracle's legacy business as it pivots.

insights.claim: In straight credit you are effectively short a 'management put' — management can re-lever or make acquisitions that break a credit thesis that looked fine on EBITDA coverage — while in convertible space you are 'straddling management' because the equity option upside makes management's discretion more symmetric.
insights.speaker: Franklin Parlamis
insights.so_what: Judgments about management capital-allocation behavior are central to convert selection; the equity-linked payoff partly offsets the short-management-put risk that plagues pure credit.

insights.claim: Traditional equity skew (vol rises as the stock falls) has clearly changed to an environment where stocks rise and vol rises with them, flattening skews; Parlamis admits he has no clean explanation but says the shift is unmistakable.
insights.speaker: Franklin Parlamis
insights.so_what: The breakdown of traditional skew changes how converts and mandatory skew profiles should be priced and hedged; up-vol behavior is now the prototypical pattern.

insights.claim: Parlamis's macro hypothesis for inverse skew: if the system defaults on debt, skew should be traditional (issuers approaching the 'sucking sound of bankruptcy' get more volatile down), but if the government monetizes debt and risks inflation, printing to prevent defaults removes downside default risk while adding upside/inflation risk, producing inverse skew.
insights.speaker: Franklin Parlamis
insights.so_what: If non-standard monetary tools are driving inverse skew, the regime persists as long as the policy backstop does — a structural, not transient, change in the shape of vol surfaces.

insights.claim: In a bubble, skew should be inverse because the upside is effectively infinite while the downside is bounded at zero; consistent with this, inverse skew is already visible in listed-option arbitrage but convertible bonds still retain some traditional skew (though maybe not worth paying for).
insights.speaker: Franklin Parlamis
insights.so_what: A residual traditional skew in converts versus inverse skew in listed options is itself a potential relative-value opportunity, and a bubble read argues the convert skew is unlikely to be worth much.

insights.claim: Oracle was the first big mandatory convertible; a mandatory pays dividends for three years then mandatorily converts to stock, with a 'flat zone' that returns par within a ~25% range, a minimum share count to the upside and a maximum to the downside — making it effectively long a call at one strike and short a put at a lower strike (a pure skew instrument).
insights.speaker: Franklin Parlamis
insights.so_what: Mandatories are a distinct discipline from ordinary converts — a stock forward plus a concentrated skew position — and, with a 3-year-into-2-year LEAP-like term, are more directly arbitrageable against listed options.

insights.claim: Because mandatories concentrate large long and short gamma at specific strikes, the position itself can move the underlying: when the market is long too much gamma at a strike, realized vol dampens there as everyone monetizes gamma, and when short too much, vol is enhanced at that strike.
insights.speaker: Franklin Parlamis
insights.so_what: Size a mandatory against the stock's average daily volume and issuer size; the stock's price action near key strikes may be gamma-driven rather than fundamental information.

insights.claim: To trade mandatory convertibles correctly you must sometimes disbelieve the stock's gamma-driven move and hold through it — which, in conventional terms, means you effectively have to be a 'bad risk manager' in the traditional sense.
insights.speaker: Franklin Parlamis
insights.so_what: Standard stop-loss discipline can be exactly wrong around concentrated-gamma strikes; the edge comes from fading gamma-induced moves rather than respecting them.
insights.quote: you have to basically be a bad risk manager to trade in the traditional sense to trade mandatory conversals correctly.

themes.theme: Black-Scholes critique and custom forward distributions

insights.claim: For a three-year 60-vol name there should be essentially no skew between the 80%- and 100%-moneyness options, because such a stock's true forward distribution is bimodal (like a roulette bet that ends at 0 or 200, not a bell curve centered up 15%) — and skew is only worth anything if the stock lingers near a strike, which these names won't.
insights.speaker: Franklin Parlamis
insights.so_what: Arguing over the 'right' skew on a high-vol name is largely wasted effort; a bimodal forward view says that skew carries almost no Vega by expiry, so don't overpay or overhedge it.

insights.claim: Parlamis advocates abandoning the Black-Scholes stochastic treatment for high-vol names in favor of pricing options in dollar/break-even terms and drawing custom forward probability distributions — a 'lava lamp' redistribution tool (essentially a Monte Carlo valuation) that he says AI could now easily build.
insights.speaker: Franklin Parlamis
insights.so_what: A pragmatic scenario/break-even valuation of long-dated options is likely to beat arguing implied-vol skew levels; the tooling gap is now trivial to close with AI.

themes.theme: Convert community as alpha and forward view

insights.claim: Aequim views alpha as coming partly from being embedded in the tight-knit convert community's flow and positioning — connected to dealers and even competitors — pursuing 'Pareto-optimal' trading where you make money without taking it from a counterparty, contrasting sharply with the 'creditor-on-creditor violence' (uptiering) now infecting credit and distressed markets.
insights.speaker: Franklin Parlamis
insights.so_what: Relationship-driven flow and a collaborative convert culture are a real information edge; the predatory uptiering dynamic in distressed credit is a structural negative for that space.

insights.claim: Trading volatility forces humility — you must assume you are the stupidest person in the room on every single trade and can't hold too many strong views, or you won't last — which Parlamis frames as both a survival trait and the reason the convert community stays collaborative.
insights.speaker: Franklin Parlamis
insights.so_what: Persistent humility and view-minimization is the behavioral edge in vol trading; overconfidence is the fastest route out of the game.
insights.quote: you have to assume that you're the stupidest person in the room on every single trade.

insights.claim: There is an open debate in converts — veterans complain implied vols are higher than anything ever paid, while the next generation argues a paradigm shift has made single-name vol permanently higher — and a five-year shift is effectively permanent for most careers, with converts stuck in a positive feedback loop where 'flow begets profit begets flows.'
insights.speaker: Franklin Parlamis
insights.so_what: Whether elevated single-name vol is permanent is the central unresolved question; the self-reinforcing flow-profit-flow loop can sustain rich vol far longer than fundamentals justify.
insights.quote: the next generation suggests that we have experienced a paradigm shift where single name uh volatility has moved permanently higher

insights.claim: Parlamis's answer to not knowing whether single-name vol is permanently higher is to refuse the binary and instead build a portfolio of asymmetric trades — each roughly 'make two, lose one' in both possible worlds — so the book profits regardless of which regime prevails, the hallmark of a true arbitrage portfolio.
insights.speaker: Franklin Parlamis
insights.so_what: Market-neutral positioning means constructing asymmetric bets against each prevailing macro call rather than making a directional vol-regime forecast — a repeatable way to trade radical uncertainty.

insights.claim: The host (Dean Curnutt) argues the accelerating pace of change in markets makes long-convexity products like convertibles attractive, but stresses that nothing is a win at any price — you still have to buy the convexity at the right price.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Long-convexity exposure is a favored structural stance amid faster market change, but valuation discipline still governs whether converts are actually a good buy.
